canonical_definition: In the provided literature, Ambiguity is framed in two distinct ways. One common framing, also referred to as Subjectivity, defines it as the degree to which a summary claim allows for multiple reasonable interpretations, which complicates objective judgments of faithfulness. This is presented as an "inherent challenge in evaluating narrative summaries." This form of ambiguity is operationalized by measuring annotator disagreement on a faithfulness label, which serves as a proxy. A different line of work defines Ambiguity more narrowly as pronunciation or semantic confusion arising from the combination of lyrics and melody, with a stated goal to "reduce abruptness and ambiguity." The construct is frequently studied in relation to `Faithfulness`. For measurement, the `MeetingBank` dataset is used to assess Ambiguity. Additionally, one paper hypothesizes a directional relationship, suggesting that "ambiguity reduction should lead to improved code accuracy," linking it to `Task success`.
papers: Comparing Specialised Small and General Large Language Models on Text Classification: 100 Labelled Samples to Achieve Break-Even Performance
DPED: Multi-Layer Noise Distillation for Privacy-Preserving Text Embeddings
Permutative Preference Alignment from Listwise Ranking of Human Judgments

definitions.text: The degree to which a summary claim admits multiple reasonable interpretations, making faithfulness judgments less objectively agreed upon.
definitions.paper: Comparing Specialised Small and General Large Language Models on Text Classification: 100 Labelled Samples to Achieve Break-Even Performance

definitions.text: The tendency for the combination of lyrics and melody to create pronunciation or semantic confusion.
definitions.paper: Permutative Preference Alignment from Listwise Ranking of Human Judgments
